steps.title: Identify whether the name is essential
steps.content: The sentence refers to "the novelist" the editors turned to—no specific novelist has been named yet. "Stephen Graham Jones" is essential to identify which novelist is meant, making this a restrictive appositive. Restrictive appositives take no punctuation.

steps.title: Apply the rule for essential appositives
steps.content:
Rule: restrictive appositive = no commas or other punctuation separating it from the noun. Test each choice:

A) novelist Stephen Graham Jones — no punctuation between "novelist" and the name. Treats the name as essential and identifying. Correct.

B) novelist, Stephen Graham Jones, — commas on both sides treat the name as non-essential (non-restrictive), implying the reader already knows which novelist is meant. No such context exists. Fails.

C) novelist. Stephen Graham Jones — a period creates two fragments; "the editors turned to the novelist" is incomplete, and "Stephen Graham Jones" becomes a standalone fragment. Fails.

D) novelist Stephen Graham Jones, — a trailing comma after "Jones" incorrectly separates the subject from the verb "to craft," breaking the sentence. Fails.
